reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Accurately reflects the source: `commandRequiresConfig: false` is correctly surfaced as 'does not require a local config file', which is a non-obvious and practically important detail.

Error handling section is grounded in the actual error throwing (stpErrors.e77/e78) and tells the reader exactly what feedback they'll get — the 'lists all available referenceable parameters' note is genuinely useful.
The shell variable capture example (`API_URL=$(...)`) is a realistic, practical pattern that many readers will actually copy. This is the highest-value example on the page.
Four distinct, realistic example scenarios cover the main usage shapes without padding.

No forbidden patterns: no YAML Stacktape config, no StacktapeConfig type, no defineConfig in fenced blocks, no marketing language, no multi-command shell blocks, no explanatory # comments inside code blocks.
problems: The page's section order deviates from the CLI page template prescribed in section instructions: the prescribed order is intro → usage → auto-generated reference → examples → related commands. The page inserts 'When to use' before examples and places the arguments reference after examples. The reference should precede the Examples section.

The 'When to use' content is expanded into its own H2 with 4 bullet points. CLI section instructions say to fold 'when to use' into the intro paragraph ('Lead with one short paragraph stating what the command does and when you'd use it'), not promote it to a standalone section.

The page never explains how the target stack is resolved. The command has optional `stage` and `projectName` args (from `stackArgs` in commands.ts) but the reader is never told what happens when these are omitted, whether defaults from `defaults:configure` apply, or when they must be provided explicitly. A first-time user with multiple stages will be confused.

The Related commands section suggests using `compile-template` to 'see which parameters a resource exposes'. This is misleading — `compile-template` generates a CloudFormation template; Stacktape referenceable parameters are a Stacktape-layer abstraction and do not map 1:1 to CloudFormation outputs visible in that template. A better suggestion would be the referenceable parameters reference page itself.
No mention that `--projectName` may be required alongside `--stage` when multiple projects share the same stage name in a region, which is a common confusion point for new users.
optionalImprovements: Reorder sections to match the CLI template: intro paragraph → Usage → Arguments reference → Examples → Related commands. Fold the 'When to use' bullets into the intro paragraph (2-3 sentences is enough for a lightweight command like this).

Add one sentence explaining stack targeting: 'If you have configured defaults with `defaults:configure`, `--region` and `--stage` can be omitted from repeated calls. Use `--projectName` alongside `--stage` when the project name cannot be inferred from the current directory.'

Replace the `compile-template` related command with a link to the referenceable parameters reference page (`/configuration/referenceable-parameters`) which directly answers 'what parameters can I request for each resource type' — far more useful than the compiled CF template.

The intro paragraph currently reads as two separate sentences about different things. Tighten to one 40-60 word paragraph that front-loads the 'no config file needed' detail, since that's the most surprising and useful property of this command compared to most Stacktape commands.

Consider adding a FAQ section (even 3-4 questions) covering: 'What referenceable parameters does each resource type expose?', 'Do I need to be in the project directory to run param:get?', 'Can I use this in CI/CD scripts?' — these are natural first-timer questions for a lookup utility.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Error handling section is concretely grounded in the source (stpErrors.e77 and stpErrors.e78 behavior accurately described — including that e78 lists the available referenceable params for the resource type).
The variable-capture bash example (`API_URL=$(stacktape param:get ...)`) is a realistic, high-value scripting pattern that most CLI reference pages omit.
No style violations: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no marketing language, one command per bash block, no inline # comments used as prose.
Uses `<CliCommandsApiReference>` correctly with all args from the source populated.
Related commands are well-chosen and linked correctly (info:stack, compile-template, defaults:configure).
The note that no local config file is required is sourced and accurate (commandRequiresConfig: false in the command handler).
problems: Section ordering deviates from the CLI section instructions. The instructions say: intro paragraph → usage example → auto-generated reference → Examples → Related commands. The draft inserts 'When to use', 'Examples', and 'Error handling' all BEFORE the arguments reference, pushing the reference to the bottom. This is a structural deviation from the CLI page contract.

The intro paragraph claims the command requires 'only the region, resource name, and parameter name.' This is technically correct per requiredArgs but misleading in practice — without a stage or a configured default, the command cannot identify which deployed stack to query. The framing understates the role of --stage.

The third example uses the framing 'when multiple stages share the same project name' to motivate --stage. This is wrong: --stage identifies which stack deployment to query (e.g., production vs. staging), not a disambiguation workaround for project name collisions. Any typical use of param:get against a non-default stage needs --stage.

The 'When to use' bullet list essentially restates the intro paragraph with slight elaboration. As a standalone H2 it adds page weight without new information. The CLI section instructions fold this context into the opening paragraph rather than a separate section.
mandatoryFixes: Reorder H2 sections to match the CLI section instruction contract: intro paragraph → Usage (code block) → Arguments reference (<CliCommandsApiReference>) → Examples → Related commands. Optional additions like 'When to use' and 'Error handling' may be inserted, but the Arguments reference must appear earlier — not after all examples.

Fix the --stage framing in the third example. Replace 'Use the stage flag when multiple stages share the same project name' with accurate language: '--stage identifies which deployed stack to query. Include it unless you have set a default stage via defaults:configure.' Update the intro paragraph similarly to reflect that stage (or a configured default) is usually needed alongside region to locate the target stack.
optionalImprovements: Collapse or remove the 'When to use' H2 section — merge its bullet points into the intro paragraph (per CLI section instructions) or cut them entirely since they largely restate the command description.

The first usage block under '## Usage' omits --stage. Consider adding it (with a note that defaults can be set) so the canonical copy-paste invocation actually works for the majority of users who haven't configured a default stage.

The 'Error handling' section is useful content, but its placement between Examples and Arguments reference is awkward. Consider folding it into a brief prose sentence after the intro, or retaining it as an H2 but placing it after the arguments reference so it doesn't interrupt the flow from usage to reference.

Consider adding a one-sentence callout that stage and projectName defaults can be configured with `defaults:configure` so repeated invocations don't require spelling out all flags — this is particularly relevant for scripting contexts the variable-capture example targets.

The page has no FAQ section. For a CLI reference page this is lower priority than for resource pages, but a 3-4 entry FAQ covering 'How do I discover which parameter names a resource exposes?', 'Do I need a config file to run param:get?', and 'Can I use param:get in CI?' would capture common search queries and help LLM citation engines.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The page explains the core purpose of `param:get` clearly and keeps examples realistic and copyable.
All shell examples use one command per fenced block and avoid explanatory comments inside copyable commands.
The page uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" />` component instead of hand-authoring the flags table.
The examples cover common uses: endpoint lookup, database connection string retrieval, stage selection, and shell-variable capture.
problems: The opening paragraph is only about 36 words, below the required 40-75 word answer-first target for extractability.
The page order drifts from the CLI page instruction: the generated arguments reference should appear after the usage example and before additional examples.

The statement that the command needs "only the region, resource name, and parameter name" is too absolute. The source proves those are required args, but stack selection can also involve optional stack args such as `stage`, `projectName`, `stackName`, defaults, or account/profile selection.

The error-handling section describes detailed error behavior, but the source list does not include `src/config/error-messages.ts`; the command source only proves `e77` and `e78` are thrown with certain fields.

The related command `compile-template` is a weaker sibling for this command than `info:stack`; the source only says `compile-template` compiles a CloudFormation template, not that it is the right way to discover referenceable parameters.
optionalImprovements: Expand the first paragraph to 40-75 words while keeping it self-contained.
Move `## Arguments reference` directly after `## Usage`, then put `## Examples` after the generated reference to match the CLI page structure.

Reword the local-config sentence to: `The command is marked as not requiring a Stacktape configuration file; it still needs enough stack-identifying arguments or configured defaults for Stacktape to find the deployed stack.`

Make the error section source-grounded by saying the command throws an error when the resource is missing and a separate error when the parameter is missing; only mention the available-parameters list if `src/config/error-messages.ts` is provided or cited.
Consider linking `referenceable parameter` again near the `paramName` examples so readers can discover valid parameter names without relying on error output.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the provided CLI command definition and implementation. The main factual risk is that a few examples and wording go beyond the supplied sources by naming specific referenceable parameter names and describing exact error-message rendering that is delegated to error definitions not included here.

issues.statement: "If the parameter name is not valid for the given resource type, the error message lists all available referenceable parameters for that resource."
issues.reason: The command implementation passes the available referenceable parameter names into `stpErrors.e78`, but the provided sources do not include the error-message definition, so they do not prove that the rendered error message lists those values to the user.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to the implementation: "If the parameter name is not valid for the resource, the command fails with `e78`; the command passes the resource type and available referenceable parameter names into the error handler." Or remove the claim about the exact rendered message unless `src/config/error-messages.ts` is provided.

issues.statement: The examples use concrete parameter names `url`, `connectionString`, and `distributionDomainName` for resources named `api`, `mainDatabase`, and `cdn`.
issues.reason: The provided source for `param:get` documents only that the command retrieves a named referenceable parameter from a deployed resource. It does not include the resource referenceable-parameter catalog proving these specific parameter names for these resource types.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide the referenceable-parameter source for these resource types, or make the examples more generic with placeholders like `--paramName <parameter-name>` and point readers to `/configuration/referenceable-parameters`.

positiveFindings: The command name, route, and required arguments match `commandDefinitions['param:get']`.
The statement that `param:get` does not require a local Stacktape configuration file is grounded by `commandRequiresConfig: false` in the command implementation.
The `<CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" ... />` component uses the correct command name for this CLI page.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and do not include copy-buffer comments.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the command definition and runtime implementation for `param:get`, including required arguments and the use of `<CliCommandsApiReference>`. I found a few unsupported or overstated claims, mainly around stack selection behavior, config-file requirements, error-message detail, and example parameter names that are not grounded in the provided source list.

issues.statement: `It does not require a local Stacktape configuration file — only the region, resource name, and parameter name.`
issues.reason: The runtime implementation does set `commandRequiresConfig: false`, and the command definition requires `region`, `resourceName`, and `paramName`. However, `configPath` is still an allowed optional argument and stack selection may also use optional stack args such as `stage` or `projectName`. Saying it requires only those three values overstates what the provided sources prove about how the command identifies the target stack.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to: `The command does not require a local Stacktape configuration file. The required CLI arguments are --region, --resourceName, and --paramName; optional stack-selection arguments such as --stage or --projectName can still be used when needed.`

issues.statement: `Use the stage flag when multiple stages share the same project name:` followed by `stacktape param:get --region eu-west-1 --stage production --resourceName cdn --paramName distributionDomainName`.
issues.reason: The source shows `stage` is an optional argument via `...stackArgs`, but the provided files do not document the specific condition that `--stage` is needed when multiple stages share the same project name. That may be true elsewhere, but it is not grounded in the provided source list.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the lead-in to a narrower, source-grounded sentence such as: `Pass --stage when you want to target a specific stage explicitly.`

issues.statement: `If the resource name does not exist in the deployed stack, the command fails with an error identifying the stack and resource.`
issues.reason: The implementation calls `stpErrors.e77` with `stackName` and `resourceName`, but the provided source does not include `src/config/error-messages.ts` or the definition of `e77`. The draft claims the visible error message identifies both values, which is not verifiable from the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Write only what the command implementation proves: `If the resource name does not exist in the deployed stack overview, the command throws the missing-resource error for that stack/resource lookup.` Or cite the exact error text only if `error-messages.ts` is provided.

issues.statement: `If the parameter name is not valid for the given resource type, the error message lists all available referenceable parameters for that resource — making it easy to discover the correct name without checking documentation.`
issues.reason: The implementation passes `referenceableParams: Object.keys(resource.referencableParams)` to `stpErrors.e78`, but the provided files do not include the error-message formatter. The source supports that the error receives the available parameter names, not that the rendered message lists them or that documentation is unnecessary.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to: `If the parameter name is not found, the command throws the invalid-parameter error and passes the resource type plus the available referenceable parameter names to the error handler.`

issues.statement: The examples use specific parameter names such as `url`, `connectionString`, and `distributionDomainName`.
issues.reason: The command accepts an arbitrary `paramName`, but the provided source files do not enumerate referenceable parameters for resource types. These example parameter names may be valid, but they are not grounded in the supplied CLI source or generated `param:get` summary.
issues.suggestedFix: Either provide source files that enumerate referenceable parameters, or make the examples generic (`--paramName <referenceable-param-name>`) and point readers to `/configuration/referenceable-parameters` for the resource-specific names.

positiveFindings: The page correctly uses the documented command name `param:get` and the source-backed description that it retrieves a specified parameter from a resource in a deployed stack.
The usage examples show the required arguments `--region`, `--resourceName`, and `--paramName`, matching `requiredArgs` in `src/config/cli/commands.ts`.
The draft uses one command per shell block and does not include shell comments inside copyable command snippets.
The `<CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" ... />` component uses the correct command name for this CLI page.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the required `param:get` arguments, uses realistic one-command shell examples, and includes the required `CliCommandsApiReference` component. I found no API-completeness, union-variant, or demonstrativeness issues under this audit scope.
positiveFindings: The page documents exactly one CLI command and uses `<CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" ... />` rather than a hand-authored flags table.
The primary usage example includes all required arguments from `commandDefinitions['param:get'].requiredArgs`: `--region`, `--resourceName`, and `--paramName`.
The draft accurately acknowledges that a local config is not required; `commandParamGet` initializes stack services with `commandRequiresConfig: false`.
Each shell snippet contains one command and avoids inline shell comments, matching the copy-button rules.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Opening paragraph is tight, self-contained, and names all three required arguments upfront — ideal for quick orientation and AI snippet extraction.
Error handling section is directly grounded in the command source (e77 and e78 from src/commands/param-get/index.ts), giving readers concrete recovery guidance without leaving the page.
Shell variable capture example (`API_URL=$(stacktape param:get ...)`) is the most practically valuable snippet on the page and exactly what a developer writing a CI step would reach for.
Correctly documents that no config file is required (`commandRequiresConfig: false`), which is a non-obvious fact that saves confusion for first-time users.
Proper use of `<CliCommandsApiReference>` per section instructions; no forbidden patterns (no hand-authored YAML, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TypeScript config blocks).
Each bash example lives in its own fenced block with prose before it — clean copy-paste behavior throughout.
FAQ appropriately scoped to a narrow CLI command: 5 quality entries beats padding to 8 synthetic ones.
problems: The Related commands section links `compile-template` with the description 'inspect the generated CloudFormation template to see which parameters a resource exposes.' CloudFormation output does not map directly to Stacktape referenceable parameter names — this misdirects readers expecting a shortcut to discover valid `--paramName` values.
The 'Common scenarios' bullet list in the Usage section duplicates information already carried by the Examples section and FAQ. It adds length without adding new signal for this audience.

FAQ mix leans heavily Stacktape-specific (4 of 5 questions). There are no use-case or choosing questions about underlying AWS behavior (e.g., 'Where does Stacktape store parameter values? Is it SSM / CloudFormation Outputs?'), which would help readers who arrive via search.
optionalImprovements: Replace the compile-template Related command entry with `param:list` or `info:operations` if either exists, or rewrite the description to be honest: compile-template shows the CloudFormation template, not Stacktape referenceable param names. Link to /configuration/referenceable-parameters instead for param discovery.

Trim or remove the 'Common scenarios' bullet list under Usage; the same information is covered more concretely in Examples and FAQ. The tighter the lead, the faster first-time readers reach the runnable example.

Add 1-2 FAQ entries covering underlying mechanics or comparison questions, e.g.: 'Where does Stacktape store parameter values?' (answer: stack outputs / SSM, resolved at runtime by the deployed stack overview) and 'When should I use param:get vs info:stack?' (single value scripting vs full exploration). This broadens SEO surface and addresses readers arriving from AWS-specific searches.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Required arguments are correctly grounded in source: `requiredArgs: ['region', 'resourceName', 'paramName']` — the page accurately calls out all three and correctly marks `--stage` as optional.

Error handling section references the exact error codes (e77, e78) from `src/commands/param-get/index.ts` and accurately describes what each error includes — especially the e78 behavior of surfacing available parameter names.

Shell examples are one-per-block with no `#` comments inside them. Each block is introduced by a prose sentence. The shell variable capture pattern (`API_URL=$(...)`) and `--agent` flag usage are practical and directly useful.
The page correctly notes `commandRequiresConfig: false` — confirmed by the source — which is a non-obvious behavior that operators will hit immediately when scripting.
`<CliCommandsApiReference>` is used correctly for the flags table; no hand-authored flag list.
FAQ is appropriately scoped for a narrow CLI page — 5 distinct, useful Q&A pairs that address scripting, CI/CD, and comparison with `info:stack`.
problems: The Related commands section links to `compile-template` and says it helps 'inspect the generated CloudFormation template to see which parameters a resource exposes.' This is misleading. The compiled CloudFormation template exposes CF Outputs — not a directly readable list of `param:get` parameter names. A user following this tip would need to manually parse CF Outputs to discover param names, which is worse than checking the referenceable parameters docs page already linked elsewhere on the page. This could cause a user to waste time and reach wrong conclusions about what `--paramName` values are valid.

The introductory paragraph and the 'Usage' section both state 'The three required arguments are `--region`, `--resourceName`, and `--paramName`' — stated twice in close proximity, which is slightly redundant for such a short page.
optionalImprovements: Replace the `compile-template` entry in Related commands. The suggested use ('inspect the generated CloudFormation template to see which parameters a resource exposes') is not a reliable or direct way to discover `param:get` parameter names. Remove it or replace it with a direct link to the referenceable parameters page, which is already linked inline on the page but not in the Related commands block.

Remove the second instance of 'The required CLI arguments are `--region`, `--resourceName`, and `--paramName`' in the paragraph following the Usage example — it repeats information stated in the intro paragraph one sentence earlier.

Consider adding a FAQ entry for: 'What happens if I run param:get against a stack that has drifted from its configuration?' The source passes `commandModifiesStack: false` and does not redeploy — it reads from the deployed stack overview — which might surprise users who recently updated config without redeploying.

The FAQ entry 'Does param:get require a deployed stack?' could add a one-liner about what the command queries specifically (the deployed stack overview manager), which sets accurate expectations around freshness — if the stack was modified outside of Stacktape, the returned value may not reflect the current state.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, concrete, and within the 40-75 word target.
Uses the required <CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" /> component instead of hand-authoring the flags table.
Shell examples follow the one-command-per-block rule and show the required param:get flags.
Error handling is grounded in the command source: missing resources map to e77 and invalid parameters map to e78.
problems: The `Usage` H2 begins with a code block instead of a self-contained paragraph, which weakens extractability for answer engines.
The `Arguments reference` H2 begins directly with an MDX component and has no self-contained opening paragraph.
Several examples omit `--stage`, which is technically allowed but may be ambiguous for users with multiple stacks unless defaults or stack selection are already configured.
The FAQ is useful but narrow: it is mostly Stacktape-specific and has limited broader CLI/automation decision coverage.

The related command `compile-template` is a weak fit for discovering runtime parameter values because param:get reads deployed stack overview data, while compile-template inspects generated CloudFormation before deployment.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-sentence paragraph under `## Usage` before the first command, naming `param:get` and explaining the required stack/resource/parameter selection.
Add a short paragraph before `<CliCommandsApiReference />` explaining that the table is the complete generated argument reference for `param:get`.
Make one stage-explicit command the primary example, or add `--projectName` where useful, so stack targeting is clearer for multi-stage projects.
Expand the FAQ with 1-2 practical automation questions, such as when to use `--agent` versus plain stdout and how `param:get` differs from reading values from CloudFormation or `info:stack`.
Consider replacing `compile-template` in Related commands with a closer sibling such as `info:stacks` or `defaults:list`, unless source-backed navigation specifically prefers `compile-template`.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly consistent with the provided `param:get` command source. The command name, required arguments, config-independent initialization, error paths, and closest related commands are generally grounded. I found a few claims that are either too broad for the provided sources or likely to mislead CLI readers about the actual output shape.

issues.statement: “Use `--agent` to get machine-readable JSONL output, suitable for programmatic consumption or piping into other tools.” and “Use `--agent` or `--outputFormat jsonl` for machine-readable output that is easier to parse programmatically.”
issues.reason: The command implementation returns the raw parameter value and only prints the TUI success/value output when invoked from the CLI. The provided `param:get` implementation does not show command-specific JSONL payload structure for `--agent` or `--outputFormat jsonl`, so the draft overstates that this command returns a useful machine-readable JSONL representation of the parameter value. The global argument description says agent mode optimizes output generally, but the command source only proves the return value is a string.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the recommendation: say `param:get` returns the parameter value as a string from the command handler, and use `--agent` only if you need the CLI's global agent-mode output behavior. Avoid implying a documented JSONL schema for `param:get` unless the page has source for it.

issues.statement: “Since `param:get` prints the parameter value to stdout, you can capture it in a shell variable…”
issues.reason: The CLI branch prints a success line and then the bold parameter value through `tuiManager`, so the provided source does not support the stronger claim that stdout contains only the parameter value in normal CLI mode. Capturing the example command may capture formatting or status output depending on the global output mode.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the scripting guidance to use the command handler behavior cautiously, for example: “For scripting, prefer a non-interactive output mode and verify the emitted format for your CLI version before capturing it.” If the docs want a copyable capture example, pair it with a supported plain or agent output mode only if source confirms the exact output.

issues.statement: “Retrieve the runtime value of a referenceable parameter…” and “Verify the actual deployed value of a parameter after a deploy completes.”
issues.reason: The source shows `param:get` reads `param.value` from `deployedStackOverviewManager.stackInfoMap.resources[resourceName].referencableParams`, but it does not show whether those values are always live runtime reads from AWS or stored stack overview data. “Runtime value” and “actual deployed value” may be accurate, but the source provided supports narrower wording.
issues.suggestedFix: Use source-shaped wording: “Retrieve the stored value of a referenceable parameter from the deployed stack overview.”

issues.statement: “If you need a broader overview of all stack outputs and resources at once, use `info:stack` instead.” and FAQ says `info:stack` “returns a broader overview of all outputs and AWS resources in the stack.”
issues.reason: The `info:stack` command description supports detailed stack information, outputs, and resources, but the word “all” is broader than the source wording. This is low impact, but narrowing avoids promising exhaustive coverage not shown by the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace “all outputs and resources” with “stack outputs and the list of AWS resources in the stack,” matching the command description.

positiveFindings: The documented required arguments match `requiredArgs: ['region', 'resourceName', 'paramName']` in `src/config/cli/commands.ts`.
The claim that `param:get` does not require a local Stacktape configuration file is grounded by `commandRequiresConfig: false` in `src/commands/param-get/index.ts`.

The error-handling section is well grounded: the source throws `e77` when the resource is missing and `e78` with the resource type plus available referenceable parameter names when the parameter is missing.
The page uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" ... />` component and does not include forbidden Stacktape YAML or fenced TypeScript config examples.
All internal links used in the draft point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly grounded in the provided CLI sources: it uses the right command, required arguments, component, and related command routes. I found two concrete issues where the prose overstates behavior beyond what the source supports or contradicts the command's required-argument metadata.

issues.statement: “If you have configured defaults for region or stage via defaults:configure, those values apply automatically and can be omitted from the invocation.”
issues.reason: For `param:get`, `region` is explicitly listed as a required argument. The provided `defaults:configure` description says defaults can be set, but the source provided here does not show that those defaults satisfy `param:get` required-argument validation. The statement tells readers they can omit `--region`, which contradicts the command metadata used for the CLI reference.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the examples showing `--region`. Narrow the sentence to stage/project defaults only if needed, or write: “`--stage` is optional; pass it when you need to target a specific stage explicitly.” Do not say `--region` can be omitted unless the defaults-resolution source is provided.

issues.statement: “Since `param:get` prints the parameter value to stdout, you can capture it in a shell variable…”
issues.reason: The command prints a success message before printing the parameter value when invoked from the CLI. Capturing stdout may include more than just the value depending on how `tuiManager.success` writes output, so the prose overstates the output shape. The implementation does return the parameter value from `commandParamGet`, but the CLI branch also emits formatted terminal output.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: “In the normal terminal output, `param:get` prints the retrieved value after a success message. For automation, prefer `--agent` or `--outputFormat jsonl` when you need machine-readable output.”

positiveFindings: The page correctly identifies the command as `param:get` and matches the source description: it retrieves a specified parameter from a resource in a deployed stack.
The required arguments `--region`, `--resourceName`, and `--paramName` match `commandDefinitions['param:get'].requiredArgs`.
The draft correctly states that the command does not require a local Stacktape configuration file; the implementation initializes deployed-stack services with `commandRequiresConfig: false`.
The error-handling section is grounded: missing resources throw `e77`, and missing parameters throw `e78` with the resource type and available referenceable parameter names.
All internal links in the draft point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.
The CLI reference uses `<CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" ... />`, which matches the command name in source.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly complete for the `param:get` CLI surface. It documents the command’s required arguments, uses the required `<CliCommandsApiReference command="param:get" />` component, and the examples demonstrate the command with all required flags shown. I did not find API-completeness, union-variant, or demonstrativeness issues that should block publication.
positiveFindings: The page documents exactly one CLI command and uses the required auto-generated CLI reference component.
All required arguments from `commandDefinitions['param:get'].requiredArgs` are shown in the usage examples: `--region`, `--resourceName`, and `--paramName`.
The draft correctly states that the command does not require a local Stacktape configuration file, matching `commandRequiresConfig: false` in `src/commands/param-get/index.ts`.
The error-handling section is grounded in the implementation paths for missing resources (`e77`) and missing parameters (`e78`).

pipelineReviewSummary: reviewer-avg 7.9, hard blockers 1, advisory high 0, verifier medium 3
